workload_identity_storage_bindings.conclusion: GCS bucket exists and env is configured, but worker IAM is not sufficient for GCS router use today.

2_workload_analysis.retention_need: Current evidence points to rare, modest spillover. Cross-pod restart durability is already covered by SeaweedFS. Cross-cluster or disaster recovery requires GCS.

3_decision_doc.recommended_option: GCS for GKE production cloud spill tier; keep SeaweedFS as local/kind and in-cluster object store
rationale: GKE is already in GCP and uses Workload Identity successfully for other services.
GCS bucket noetl-demo-output already exists, but worker IAM must be granted before flipping.
Current spillover volume is small enough that storage cost is not the deciding factor.
SeaweedFS is good for pod restart durability but is not cross-cluster disaster recovery.
Remote AWS S3 adds cross-cloud egress and credential complexity without a current requirement.

4_close_out.implementation_round: GKE storage cloud tier to GCS

verdict: Recommend GCS as the durable GKE production cloud tier once bucket IAM is granted. SeaweedFS remains the right in-cluster object-store backend, but not the cross-cluster durability answer.
notes: Design-only round: no buckets, IAM, Helm values, router code, or deployments were changed.
The GCS bucket exists, but the worker service account currently receives 403 on bucket metadata access.
The live S3 cloud tier is in-cluster SeaweedFS, not remote AWS S3.
deferred_followups: Implement chosen cloud tier option (separate round): grant GCS IAM, flip NOETL_STORAGE_CLOUD_TIER=gcs, restart, and run large-result durability smoke.
Keep SeaweedFS deployed for local/kind and in-cluster S3-compatible workflows.
Do not introduce remote AWS S3 unless a new cross-cloud requirement appears.
